A Dyson swarm of billions of solar panels orbits a blue giant star in concentric shells, each panel no larger than a city block from this distance, the star's light fractured into geometric shadows across nearby moons.

Corroded megastructure panels spiral loosely around a dying red dwarf, ancient stellar collector half-dismantled by time, massive gaps revealing the star's crimson glow while derelict maintenance drones drift between the corroded segments like metallic snow.

Ringworld bands wrap a gas giant's equator in five parallel strips, each ring's interior surface dotted with pinprick city lights, storm systems visible swirling across the habitable face, tiny atmospheric processors venting clouds at the edges.

Ancient alien ring structure encircles a neutron star, crystalline material refracting the star's radiation into impossible colors, entire sections collapsed inward, the debris field containing chunks larger than Earth's moon slowly spiraling down.

Space elevator rises from a desert planet, its carbon-composite tether vanishing into the cloud layer and beyond, surface anchor city sprawling for hundreds of kilometers around the base, cargo pods ascending like luminous pearls on an infinite string.

Battle-damaged orbital tether connects ocean world to its moon, massive sections torn away revealing the internal structure, debris field orbiting alongside it, emergency repair drones swarming the breaks like phosphorescent insects against the blue planet below.

Rotating habitat station stretches fifty kilometers across, eight massive rings spinning at different speeds around a central axis, thousands of ships docked along the outer surface like barnacles, viewing ports glowing with interior city lights.

Ancient toroidal station orbits a black hole's accretion disk, polished alloy surface reflecting the gravitational lensing, entire sections dark and depressurized, the functional quarters glowing defiantly against the cosmic engine's radiation, time-dilated and slowly dying.

Generation ship the size of Manhattan drifts between stars, its hull a patchwork of repaired meteor impacts, bio-domes visible through transparent sections containing forests and lakes, fusion torch dark for decades, coasting on momentum and hope.

Worldship carved from a hollowed asteroid passes through a nebula, living bio-metal hull breathing and self-repairing, interior landscape visible through the massive observation belt, population of eight million visible as glittering city networks inside the sphere.

Orbital shipyard frames stretch across a hundred kilometers of space, three planet-killer dreadnoughts in various stages of assembly, construction drones swarming like luminous plankton, the skeletal frames dwarfing the completed escort ships docked nearby for scale.

Drydock facility built into a moon's crater, the crater walls themselves converted to construction gantries, a generation ship in pieces across the workspace, each component larger than most stations, welding flares visible as distant stars from this angle.

Defense platform bristles with weapon arrays across its ice-armored surface, each gun emplacement the size of a skyscraper, battle-scarred hull plating replaced in mismatched sections, positioned between two moons like a steel moon itself, sensor dishes tracking everything.

Fortress station guards a warp gate, eight massive weapon spires extending from the central sphere, point-defense systems covering every surface like technological coral, scorch marks from previous battles visible across the ceramic-plated hull, surrounded by a graveyard of debris.

Planet-cracking mining rig straddles a shattered world, extraction beams the width of mountain ranges cutting into the exposed mantle, ore processing refineries visible as glowing industrial complexes along the rig's spine, fragments of planetary crust drifting in controlled orbits.

Mining megastructure consumes a metal-rich asteroid field, the central hub volcanic rock fused with industrial machinery, tentacle-like conveyor systems extending hundreds of kilometers to feeding stations, the processed slag ejected as a glowing river visible against the starfield.

Communications array floats in deep space, its primary dish two hundred kilometers across, secondary antenna farms spreading like crystalline wings, relay stations positioned at lagrange points around it, the structure catching starlight like a cosmic spiderweb of reception.

Ancient alien transmission hub orbits a pulsar, continent-sized dish arrays pointed at different galaxies, the structure itself generating auroras from the star's radiation, entire sections of the facility collapsed into geometric ruins, still broadcasting automated signals after eons.

Alien megastructure drifts through a debris field, its organic architecture grown rather than built, sections overgrown with crystalline formations that catch and refract starlight, interior chambers exposed where the hull has cracked, large enough to swallow Earth whole.

Collapsed ring structure orbits a brown dwarf, the megastructure's original purpose unknowable, segments the size of continents tumbling separately now, covered in impact craters, exotic matter still glowing faintly along the fracture lines after millions of years.

Warp gate ring hangs in empty space between stars, the aperture fifty kilometers across crackling with purple Cherenkov radiation, massive magnetic containment coils visible around the circumference, reality visibly distorted through the gate's center, ships queuing at the approach markers.

Jump gate structure anchors two black holes in binary orbit, the ring itself made of exotic matter glowing white-hot from gravitational stress, spacetime visibly rippling through the aperture, maintenance stations clinging to the outer ring dwarfed to invisibility by scale.

O'Neill cylinder rotates in lunar orbit, interior landscape visible through transparent sections running its length, weather patterns forming inside the habitat, cities and farmland curved up the walls, the entire structure thirty kilometers long, cap-mounted mirrors directing sunlight inward.

Habitat cylinder under construction floats beside a gas giant, half the interior surface installed showing geometric patterns, construction ships moving along the incomplete sections like mites, the finished areas already showing green vegetation growth, transparent aluminum panels waiting in orbital staging.

Stellar plasma collector extends vast heat-shielded scoops into a yellow star's corona, magnetic containment fields glowing blue against the star's orange surface, the structure itself larger than Jupiter, thermal radiator fins extending thousands of kilometers into space, bleeding excess heat into void.
